questions.text: Which one of the following is a useful functional association between fungi and the roots of higher plants?
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: Mycorrhiza (D)**

Mycorrhiza is a mutualistic symbiotic association between fungi (mostly Glomeromycota) and plant roots, where fungi enhance nutrient (especially phosphorus) and water uptake via extensive hyphal networks, while receiving carbohydrates from the plant. Over 80% of terrestrial plants form this association. DRDO CEPTAM-11 relevance: critical for biofertilizer development in defence agriculture (sustainable farming in border/high-altitude areas like Ladakh/Siachen), soil remediation at testing ranges, and biomimicry for advanced water/nutrient delivery systems in arid military operations.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• **Biofertilizer (A):** Broad term (includes Rhizobium/Azolla); mycorrhiza is a specific type.
• **Coralloid root (B):** Cycas-specific algal symbiosis (nitrogen fixation).
• **Lichens (C):** Fungus-alga symbiosis on rocks/bark, not root association.

questions.text: Nitrogen fixing bacteria present in the nodules of the root of leguminous plant are
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: Symbiotic (A)**

Rhizobium (and Bradyrhizobium) bacteria form symbiotic nodules on legume roots (e.g., pea, soybean), fixing atmospheric N₂ into ammonia via nitrogenase enzyme in exchange for plant sugars. This reduces chemical fertilizer use by 50-100 kg/ha. DRDO context: vital for low-input sustainable agriculture in remote defence farms, bio-remediation of nitrogen-deficient soils at firing ranges, and soldier nutrition security (protein-rich pulses).

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• **Protolytic (B):** Protein-degrading bacteria (e.g., in soil decomposition).
• **Saprophytic (C):** Free-living decomposers (no symbiosis).
• **Parasitic (D):** Harmful to host (e.g., pathogens).

questions.text: Peanut plant is
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: Herb (A)**

Peanut (Arachis hypogaea) is an annual herbaceous legume (Fabaceae) with geocarpic fruit development (pegs bury into soil). DRDO relevance: high-protein oilseed crop for soldier rations (energy-dense, shelf-stable), soil nitrogen enrichment in rotation farming, and oil for defence lubricants/biodiesel research.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• **Flower (B):** Reproductive part, not plant type.
• **Bush (C):** Woody perennial growth form.
• **None of these (D):** Incorrect; it fits herb category.

questions.text: Stem cutting is done generally to grow
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: Sugarcane (B)**

Sugarcane (Saccharum officinarum) is propagated vegetatively by stem cuttings (setts) containing 2-3 nodes, ensuring clonal uniformity and rapid multiplication. DRDO applications: bioethanol production for defence fuel (green energy for vehicles), fibre for camouflage netting, and sustainable farming in tropical border regions.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• **Banana (A):** Suckers/rhizome propagation.
• **Mango (C):** Grafting/air-layering.
• **Cotton (D):** Seeds.

questions.text: Clove is a
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: Closed buds (C)**

Clove (Syzygium aromaticum) is the dried unopened flower bud (clove bud) rich in eugenol (essential oil). DRDO uses: natural analgesic/antiseptic in soldier first-aid kits, flavouring in rations, and eugenol-based mosquito repellents for vector control in field deployments.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• **Buds of stem (A):** Incorrect (floral, not stem).
• **Bales of root (B):** No relation.
• **Seed (D):** Cloves are not seeds.

questions.text: Which one of the following spices is a flower bud?
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: (B) Clove**

This question is a variation of the previous one, highlighting that **Clove is the most common example of a flower bud used as a spice**. In competitive exams like **DRDO CEPTAM-11**, knowing the **botanical identity** of the other options is just as important.

------------------------------

**Analysis of the Options**

To help you prepare, here is the breakdown of what each of those spices actually is:

**(A) Cumin (Jeera)**

• **Botanical Part:** Seed (technically the dried fruit, but classified as a seed in exams)
• **Scientific Name:** *Cuminum cyminum*
• **Key Fact:** Belongs to the **Apiaceae family** (same as carrot and parsley). Known for aiding digestion.

**(B) Clove (Laung)**

• **Botanical Part:** Flower Bud (unopened)
• **Scientific Name:** *Syzygium aromaticum*
• **Key Fact:** Contains **Eugenol**, which has antiseptic properties. It is harvested when the buds turn pink.

**(C) Pepper (Kali Mirch)**

• **Botanical Part:** Fruit (specifically a dried **drupe / berry**)
• **Scientific Name:** *Piper nigrum*
• **Key Fact:** Known as the **"King of Spices"**. The heat comes from a compound called **Piperine**. Black, white, and green peppercorns all come from the same plant but are processed differently.

**(D) Turmeric (Haldi)**

• **Botanical Part:** Rhizome (**Modified Underground Stem**)
• **Scientific Name:** *Curcuma longa*
• **Key Fact:** Active compound is **Curcumin**. Turmeric acts as a **natural indicator**, turning **reddish-brown when reacting with a base** like soap.

------------------------------

**Quick Revision Table: Plant Parts used as Spices**

This table covers the most likely questions you will face in the **General Science section**:

| Spice | Botanical Classification | Key Chemical |
|---|---|---|
| Clove | Flower Bud | Eugenol |
| Saffron | Stigma (Flower part) | Crocin |
| Cinnamon | Inner Bark | Cinnamaldehyde |
| Ginger | Rhizome (Stem) | Gingerol |
| Cardamom | Fruit / Seed | Terpinene |
| Vanilla | Fruit (Pod) | Vanillin |

------------------------------

**Why these questions are asked in DRDO CEPTAM**

These questions test your **observation of daily life science**. DRDO often looks for candidates who understand the **practical application of biology and chemistry**. For example, knowing that **Turmeric is a stem (rhizome) and not a root** is a classic **"trap" question** designed to filter out students who memorize facts without understanding.

**Next Step:** Would you like to try a few questions on **Human Systems** (Digestive or Respiratory system), or should we continue with **Economic Botany**?

questions.text: ‘Cloves’ are
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: Dried flowers (D)**

Cloves are dried flower buds (not fully opened flowers). DRDO relevance: eugenol-rich oil for pain relief in combat medicine and natural preservative in packaged foods for troops.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• **Nodules (A,B):** Incorrect (no nodules).
• **Leaves (C):** Clove leaves yield oil but spice is buds.

questions.text: Cloves are obtained from which of the following parts of the plant?
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: Dried flower buds (D)**

Unopened flower buds of Syzygium aromaticum are sun-dried to form commercial cloves. DRDO uses: antimicrobial properties in wound dressings and flavour masking in emergency rations.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• Others are not the commercial part.

questions.text: Which of the following is not correctly matched?
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: Saffron - Seed (B)**

Saffron is dried stigmas (style) of Crocus sativus flower, not seed. Others correct. DRDO context: saffron cultivation trials in high-altitude Ladakh for soldier nutrition (antioxidant-rich) and economic botany research.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• Ginger rhizome (A), opium capsule latex (C), jute stem fibre (D): correct.

questions.text: Saffron is dried mixture of
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: Seed forming parts of flower (C)**

Saffron comprises dried red stigmas + styles (female reproductive parts). World's costliest spice. DRDO relevance: anti-depressant/immune booster for high-altitude troops and potential in radiation protection research.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• Others incorrect.

questions.text: In the following pairs of plants and their edible parts, which pair is not correctly matched?
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: Potato - Roots (C)**

Potato is stem tuber; sweet potato is root. DRDO context: potato storage/processing for defence rations (high-calorie, long-shelf-life). Tests precise organ modification knowledge.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• Ginger rhizome (A), onion bulb leaves (B), coconut endosperm (D): correct.

questions.text: Which of the following parts of potato is edible?
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: Stem (A)**

Edible potato tuber is modified underground stem (with buds/eyes). DRDO relevance: vegetative propagation for rapid multiplication in forward-area farms.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• Others not edible.

questions.text: Which one of the following is a fruit?
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: Lady finger (C)**

Lady finger (okra, Abelmoschus esculentus) is a capsule fruit. Potato/radish/sweet potato: underground storage organs (not fruits). DRDO context: vegetable crops for nutritional security in military canteens.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• Potato (A): stem tuber; Radish/Sweet potato (B,D): roots.

questions.text: A plant bud is
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: An embryonic shoot (A)**

Bud contains meristematic tissue (embryonic shoot) for new growth. DRDO relevance: understanding dormancy in high-altitude crops and tissue culture for rapid propagation.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• Embryonic leaf (B): not bud definition.

questions.text: Cork is obtained from which of the following plant?
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: Quercus (C)**

Cork from Quercus suber (cork oak) bark phellem layer (suberin-rich). DRDO uses: insulation in helmets/vehicles, buoyancy in naval equipment, and eco-friendly packaging.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• Dalbergia (A): rosewood; Cedrus (B): cedar wood; Argemone (D): poppy.

questions.text: The characteristic odour of garlic is due to
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: A sulphur compound (B)**

Allicin (organosulphur) from alliin via alliinase enzyme. DRDO relevance: natural antibiotic/antifungal for soldier wound care and flavouring in rations.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• Others unrelated to garlic.

questions.text: Peeling of onions causes tears as onions release
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: Sulfenic acid (B)**

Cuts release alliinase forming syn-propanethial-S-oxide (lachrymatory factor from sulfenic acid). DRDO context: chemical irritant studies and tear gas research.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• Sulphonic (A): different; Carbolic (D): phenol.

questions.text: Pungency in chillies is due to the presence of
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: Capsaicin (B)**

Capsaicin (alkaloid) in placenta binds TRPV1 receptors causing heat sensation. DRDO uses: capsaicin-based non-lethal sprays for crowd control and pain management creams.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• Lycopene/Carotene (A,C): pigments; Anthocyanin (D): colour.

questions.text: Red chillies are hot because these contain
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: Capsaicin (B)**

Same as above; Scoville scale measures heat. DRDO relevance: defence-grade irritants and topical analgesics for troops.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• Ricin (A): castor toxin; Arecoline (C): betel nut.

questions.text: At which stage in its life cycle, does the silkworm yield the fibre of commerce?
questions.solution:
✅ **Correct Answer: Pupa (C)**

Silk fibre from Bombyx mori cocoon (pupal stage). DRDO context: sericulture for parachute silk, surgical sutures, and bullet-proof vest composites.

**Option-wise Analysis:**
• Larva (B): feeds; Imago (D): adult moth.
